title: Helicopter Manufacturing Plant Simulation
summary: Designed and simulated a helicopter assembly factory in Siemens Tecnomatix, combining production flow definition, bill-of-materials design, and cost analysis to study operational feasibility.
highlights: Designed a helicopter assembly factory using Siemens Tecnomatix.
Defined production steps and factory flow for the assembly system.
Built a bill of materials and cost-analysis layer to assess profitability.

sections.heading: Factory as system
body: The project framed helicopter assembly as a manufacturing-system design problem rather than as a single line-balancing exercise. The main task was to define how the factory should flow, what the production steps were, and how the assembly process should be structured spatially and operationally.
That required treating the plant as an integrated sequence of operations instead of a set of disconnected workstations.

sections.heading: Simulation environment
body: Siemens Tecnomatix was used to model the factory and visualize the production process. The use of a manufacturing simulation environment made it possible to reason about factory flow, staging, and work progression more concretely than a spreadsheet-only workflow would allow.
This gave the project a direct manufacturing-systems flavor inside the IIT Madras portfolio.

sections.heading: Economic layer
body: The work also included a bill of materials and a cost analysis so the plant design could be evaluated economically. That shifted the project from pure layout or automation planning toward a more decision-oriented factory study.
Profitability analysis made the output more complete because it linked production structure to financial viability.

sections.heading: Outcome
body: The final result was a manufacturing case study that combined process mapping, simulation, and cost reasoning. It reflects the broader thread of taking engineering design beyond component-level work into system-level industrial planning.
It also set up a useful bridge to later facility and operations-design work.
